key_insight: A fair head-to-head comparison shows that the best inverse-solver choice depends mainly on how the unknown is represented: grid-based fields favor adjoint methods, while neural representations favor PINNs.
engineering_relevance: For PDE-constrained parameter identification in mechanics and fluids, the result helps practitioners choose solvers based on model parameterization, cost, and whether the target is a field, closure model, or constitutive law.
method: Common formulation with matched domain, PDE, observations, regularization, optimizer, and precision
Benchmarking on Burgers, Darcy, Allen--Cahn, and Navier--Stokes inverse problems
PINN-warm-started adjoint strategy to combine lower-cost reconstruction with adjoint-level accuracy
evidence_signal: The paper evaluates both approaches on four inverse-problem benchmarks under identical conditions and reports that representation choice strongly affects accuracy-cost tradeoffs, with PINNs often cheaper for time-dependent cases.
watch_item: Performance depends on careful matching of parameterization and optimization settings, and adjoint methods can become expensive when trajectory storage and differentiation are large.
paper_title: Adjoint Method versus Physics-Informed Neural Networks in PDE-Constrained Inverse Problems
